---
title: "Visualization"
sidebar: api
---

Nine plotnine-based functions for sequence visualization. Every function
returns a bare `plotnine.ggplot` object — composable with `+` exactly
like ggplot2 in R. Nothing is rendered until you call `.draw()` or `.save()`.

```python
from yasqat.visualization import (
    index_plot, timeline_plot, spell_duration_plot,
    distribution_plot, entropy_plot, frequency_plot,
    modal_state_plot, mean_time_plot, parallel_coordinate_plot,
)
from plotnine import theme_bw, labs, theme, element_text
```

---

## `index_plot`

```python
def index_plot(
    pool: SequencePool,
    group: np.ndarray | list[int] | None = None,
    sort_by_group: bool = False,
) -> ggplot
```

One row per sequence, one column per time step — coloured by state.
The canonical sequence visualization (sequence index plot, SIP).

**Parameters**

| Name | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `pool` | `SequencePool` | The sequence pool to plot. |
| `group` | `array-like \| None` | Cluster labels; enables colour-coded group boundaries. |
| `sort_by_group` | `bool` | If `True`, stacks sequences by cluster assignment. |

```python
p = index_plot(pool)
p.draw()

# Sorted by cluster, with theme overrides
p = index_plot(pool, group=cluster_labels, sort_by_group=True)
p + theme_bw() + labs(title="Trajectories by cluster", x="Day", y="Customer")
```

::: {.callout-tip}
Best for small to medium pools (< 500 sequences). For large pools, use
`distribution_plot` or subsample first with `pool.sample(200)`.
:::

---

## `distribution_plot`

```python
def distribution_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
```

Stacked area chart showing the proportion of sequences in each state at
each time point. Cohort-level view; uses `Alphabet.colors` automatically.

```python
p = distribution_plot(pool)
p + labs(title="State distribution over time", x="Day", y="Proportion")
```

---

## `entropy_plot`

```python
def entropy_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
```

Cross-sectional Shannon entropy at each time point. Peaks signal moments
of maximum cohort diversity (e.g. after a policy shock).

```python
p = entropy_plot(pool)
p + labs(y="Shannon entropy", x="Time point")
```

---

## `frequency_plot`

```python
def frequency_plot(pool: SequencePool, n: int = 10) -> ggplot
```

Horizontal bar chart of the `n` most common distinct sequences.
Useful for detecting dominant patterns.

**Parameters**

| Name | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `n` | `10` | Number of top sequences to display. |

```python
p = frequency_plot(pool, n=20)
p.draw()
```

---

## `modal_state_plot`

```python
def modal_state_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
```

At each time point, shows the most common (modal) state. Provides the
"central tendency" trajectory of the cohort.

```python
p = modal_state_plot(pool)
p.draw()
```

---

## `mean_time_plot`

```python
def mean_time_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
```

Horizontal bar chart — one bar per state, showing mean time steps spent
in that state across all sequences.

```python
p = mean_time_plot(pool)
p + labs(title="Average time in state")
```

---

## `timeline_plot`

```python
def timeline_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
```

Horizontal Gantt-style chart — one row per sequence, coloured bars
representing each spell's state and duration.

```python
# Subset for readability
subset = pool[:50]
p = timeline_plot(subset)
p.draw()
```

---

## `spell_duration_plot`

```python
def spell_duration_plot(pool: SequencePool) -> ggplot
```

Distribution of spell lengths per state (density or box plots).
Reveals which states are "sticky" vs. transient.

```python
p = spell_duration_plot(pool)
p + labs(x="Spell length (time steps)", y="State")
```

---

## `parallel_coordinate_plot`

```python
def parallel_coordinate_plot(
    pool: SequencePool,
    alpha: float = 0.1,
) -> ggplot
```

One line per sequence — each axis is a time step, the line traces state
transitions. Best with a coarse alphabet (≤ 6 states) and small pools.

**Parameters**

| Name | Default | Description |
|---|---|---|
| `alpha` | `0.1` | Line transparency. Lower values reduce overplotting. |

```python
p = parallel_coordinate_plot(pool[:100], alpha=0.05)
p.draw()
```

---

## Composing with plotnine

Every function returns a bare `ggplot`. Apply any plotnine layer with `+`:

```python
from plotnine import theme_bw, theme, element_text, labs, scale_fill_manual

p = index_plot(pool, group=cluster_labels)

p + theme_bw() \
  + theme(axis_text_y=element_text(size=7)) \
  + labs(title="Customer lifecycle trajectories", x="Day", y="Customer") \
  + scale_fill_manual(values=pool.alphabet.colors)
```

---

## Saving plots

```python
p = index_plot(pool)
p.save("trajectories.png", dpi=150, width=12, height=8)
p.save("trajectories.pdf", width=12, height=8)
```

::: {.callout-note}
plotnine uses matplotlib as its rendering backend. In Jupyter, call
`p.draw()`. In scripts, call `p.save()` or assign the result of `p.draw()`
to a variable to suppress auto-display.
:::
